modules.title: What is a distributed ledger?
modules.learnerGoal: Explain why several nodes compare records and converge on one ordered state.
modules.verifiedConcept: A distributed ledger replicates records or state across participants that apply shared verification rules.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: A ledger records ordered changes; distributing it lets several participants verify and retain the same history.
modules.demonstration: Three ledger panels receive the same signed record; one altered copy turns amber while matching copies align.
modules.interaction: Route two valid records to every replica, then reject one panel whose second record was changed.

checkQuestion.prompt: What makes the altered replica detectable in this lesson?
answers: Its records no longer match the verified ordered state
It uses a different screen color

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: participants verify records and compare the resulting ordered state.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Look at record integrity and order, not popularity or decoration.
modules.recap: A distributed ledger is shared state maintained under common verification rules.
modules.simplificationNotes: Three panels stand in for a network; consensus and storage architectures differ between real systems.

modules.title: Transactions, blocks, and hash links
modules.learnerGoal: Distinguish an authorized transaction from a block and explain why parent hashes reveal changed history.
modules.verifiedConcept: Transactions request state changes; some blockchains group them into blocks linked to previous block hashes.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: A transaction is an action request, while a block packages records and commits to earlier history with a hash link.
modules.demonstration: Two transactions enter a block; changing the parent block breaks the child block's visible hash ribbon.
modules.interaction: Place three blocks in parent-hash order and remove the transaction whose signature check fails.

checkQuestion.prompt: Is every distributed ledger necessarily organized as hash-linked blocks?
answers: No, DLT is broader than blockchain
Only when it has a wallet

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: blockchain is one distributed-ledger architecture.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Remember that DLT is broader; some ledgers use other data structures.
modules.recap: Transactions request changes; blocks and hash links are one way to order and protect history.
modules.simplificationNotes: Visible ribbons represent cryptographic commitments and do not model serialization or every block field.

modules.title: Address, private key, and signature
modules.learnerGoal: Explain that a private key authorizes actions and must never be revealed to verifiers.
modules.verifiedConcept: Digital signatures let nodes verify authorization for a transaction without receiving the private signing key.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: An address can be shared, but the private key stays secret and creates signatures for reviewed actions.
modules.demonstration: A key remains behind an opaque shield while a signature seal travels with one exact payload.
modules.interaction: Match two signature seals to unchanged fictional payloads and reject a modified destination.

answers: The signed payload and public verification information

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: verification does not require exposing the secret key.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Private keys and recovery phrases must remain secret and are never lesson inputs.
modules.recap: Share addresses and signatures when appropriate; never disclose the private key.
modules.simplificationNotes: The shield and seal are metaphors; no real cryptographic material is generated or accepted.

modules.title: Confirmation and finality
modules.learnerGoal: Distinguish provisional, confirmed, and finalized outcomes across protocol families.
modules.verifiedConcept: Some protocols provide deterministic finality certificates while others provide increasing confidence as history accumulates.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: Included is not always final: each protocol defines when applications may safely rely on an outcome.
modules.demonstration: One lane gains confirmation rings while another waits for a validator certificate and then locks.
modules.interaction: Release each fictional delivery only when its lane reaches the scenario's stated acceptance rule.

checkQuestion.prompt: Can one confirmation number be treated as universal finality for every network?
answers: No, finality models and application risk policies differ

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: confirmation and finality depend on protocol assumptions and application policy.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Compare the certificate lane with the accumulating-confidence lane.
modules.recap: Check the protocol's finality rule before treating a result as irreversible.
modules.simplificationNotes: No universal time, confirmation count, or probability is shown.

modules.title: Pi apps, Pi Browser, and authentication
modules.learnerGoal: Describe the current Pi app authentication boundary without treating authentication as blockchain consensus.
modules.verifiedConcept: The current Pi SDK runs in Pi Browser and exposes authentication for supported apps; backends verify and use platform data under documented rules.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: Pi Browser hosts the app experience, while the SDK and backend coordinate authenticated platform actions.
modules.demonstration: A fictional app requests minimal scopes, receives a temporary auth result, and passes it to a server verification gate.
modules.interaction: Select only the fictional scope required by a profile greeting, then route verification to the server lane.

checkQuestion.prompt: Does app authentication validate a blockchain block?
answers: No, it establishes an app user context

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: authentication and consensus are separate processes.
modules.incorrectFeedback: The app is authenticating a user context, not participating in block consensus.
modules.recap: Pi app authentication is a developer-platform flow, not block validation.
modules.simplificationNotes: Uses fictional users and scopes and does not call Pi services or store personal data.

modules.title: Wallet experience, Testnet, and Mainnet
modules.learnerGoal: Separate wallet authorization from app authentication and keep Testnet and Mainnet actions visibly distinct.
modules.verifiedConcept: Pi's developer documentation distinguishes testing and main network environments; payment and wallet actions require explicit user-oriented flows.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: A test environment is for safe development, while a main network action has real protocol consequences and needs explicit review.
modules.demonstration: A fictional payment draft in a striped Testnet lane cannot cross into the solid Mainnet lane.
modules.interaction: Route sample-only actions to Testnet and reject a Mainnet-labeled request whose environment does not match.

checkQuestion.prompt: Should an app silently switch a reviewed Testnet action to Mainnet?
answers: No, environment and intent must remain explicit
Only if the amount is small

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: network context is part of the user's reviewed intent.
modules.incorrectFeedback: A network change can change consequences and must not be hidden.
modules.recap: Keep wallet intent and network environment explicit from request to result.
modules.simplificationNotes: Older environment wording is used only for environment separation and is cross-checked against current Open Network status.

modules.title: User-to-App payment lifecycle
modules.learnerGoal: Order the documented user-to-app payment stages and identify the client/server trust boundary.
modules.verifiedConcept: The SDK can create a user-to-app payment flow that requires user approval and server-side processing under the Platform API lifecycle.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: The app proposes a payment, the user reviews it, and the backend verifies and completes the documented lifecycle.
modules.demonstration: Five fictional cards animate in order: request, review, approval, server verification, completion.
modules.interaction: Place the lifecycle cards in order and keep the server credential card out of the client lane.

checkQuestion.prompt: Where should privileged server credentials remain?

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: privileged verification and credentials stay server-side.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Client code is inspectable; server credentials must not be placed there.
modules.recap: A payment is a reviewed lifecycle, not a one-step client-side state change.
modules.simplificationNotes: No payment is created; all users, identifiers, and units are fictional.

modules.title: App-to-User and server verification
modules.learnerGoal: Explain why a server must approve, submit, verify, and complete supported app-to-user flows according to current API guidance.
modules.verifiedConcept: The Platform API documents app-to-user payment endpoints and a server-controlled approval, submission, verification, and completion lifecycle.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: An app-to-user transfer is a controlled backend workflow whose result must be verified rather than assumed from a client animation.
modules.demonstration: A server lane advances a fictional transfer through approval, blockchain submission, verification, and completion states.
modules.interaction: Reject a transfer marked complete before its verification card reaches the server gate.

checkQuestion.prompt: Is a client success animation sufficient evidence that the documented transfer completed?
answers: No, the backend verifies the lifecycle result

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: UI feedback cannot replace server verification.
modules.incorrectFeedback: The visible client state and the verified platform result are separate.
modules.recap: Verify backend payment state before showing a final application outcome.
modules.simplificationNotes: The lesson models documented stages without network calls, values, rewards, or wallet credentials.

modules.title: Nodes, consensus, and evidence status
modules.learnerGoal: Classify Pi claims as current, historical, proposed, simplified, or needing verification.
modules.verifiedConcept: Current public developer workflows and Open Network status are documented separately from historical whitepaper architecture claims.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: A claim can be official yet historical, so its date and scope determine whether it describes the current network.
modules.demonstration: Cards from current SDK docs, the Open Network announcement, and the original whitepaper enter distinct status columns.
modules.interaction: Sort six claim cards, including a prohibited daily-tap validation statement, into evidence-status columns.

checkQuestion.prompt: What status fits a current node parameter found only in the original whitepaper?

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: historical official material does not automatically establish current implementation.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Check both the source authority and whether the claim is current.
modules.recap: Official history is valuable evidence, but current behavior needs current support.
modules.simplificationNotes: The categories are editorial evidence labels, not protocol states.

modules.title: Pi's historical SCP/FBA relationship
modules.learnerGoal: Describe the historical SCP/FBA lineage without presenting a simulation of current Pi consensus.
modules.verifiedConcept: Pi's original whitepaper describes consensus ideas derived from Stellar Consensus Protocol and Federated Byzantine Agreement.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: Historically, the Pi design described nodes choosing local trust requirements whose safe overlap supports federated agreement.
modules.demonstration: A historical-labeled three-node graph shows local slices combining into one overlapping quorum.
modules.interaction: Repair one simplified historical graph so its modeled quorums share an intersection point.

checkQuestion.prompt: Does this graph reveal Pi's current live quorum configuration?
answers: No, it is a historical simplified concept model

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: the lesson teaches lineage and concepts, not current implementation parameters.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Read the historical and simplified labels above the graph.
modules.recap: Pi's original paper links its design to SCP/FBA; current operational details still need verification.
modules.simplificationNotes: The exercise compresses SCP ballot behavior into quorum overlap and is explicitly historical.

modules.title: Quorums, slices, and overlap
modules.learnerGoal: Distinguish a node's quorum slice from a network quorum and identify unsafe disjoint quorums.
modules.verifiedConcept: In SCP, quorum slices are node-local sufficient sets; network safety requires suitable quorum intersection under the system assumptions.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: Each node chooses enough dependencies to advance, but the resulting network quorums must still overlap safely.
modules.demonstration: Local threshold petals expand into two candidate quorums; an unsafe layout splits into red and blue components.
modules.interaction: Move one fictional organization node so both candidate quorums retain a shared independent participant.

checkQuestion.prompt: Is one valid slice automatically a network quorum?
answers: No, slices are local and quorums are network-wide sets

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: local acceptance and global quorum structure are related but distinct.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Look at the local petal versus the larger network set.
modules.recap: Safe federated agreement depends on how local slices combine into intersecting quorums.
modules.simplificationNotes: Uses a small graph and does not reproduce production quorum-set evaluation or current Pi settings.

modules.title: Participation, rewards, and validation are different
modules.learnerGoal: Reject the claim that a daily Pi app tap directly validates blocks.
modules.verifiedConcept: The reviewed sources do not establish a daily app interaction as block validation, proof of work, or a consensus vote.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: App participation, reward policy, wallet actions, node operation, and consensus validation are separate processes.
modules.demonstration: Five labeled lanes remain separate; the daily-app card never enters the node-consensus lane.
modules.interaction: Route fictional actions to App, Reward policy, Wallet, Node, or Consensus and reject two unsupported shortcuts.

checkQuestion.prompt: What does tapping the daily app button prove about a block?
answers: Nothing by itself; it must not be presented as block validation

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: the daily interaction is not evidence of block validation.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Do not merge the app-participation lane with node or consensus behavior.
modules.recap: Daily participation is not direct block validation.
modules.simplificationNotes: The lesson states an evidence boundary and does not speculate about current reward calculations.

modules.title: UTXO and account models
modules.learnerGoal: Compare whole-output consumption with direct account-state updates.
modules.verifiedConcept: Bitcoin-style UTXO transactions consume explicit unspent outputs, while Ethereum-style account transactions mutate addressable account state in order.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: UTXO systems replace discrete outputs; account systems update ordered state under accounts.
modules.demonstration: The left lane consumes two output gems and creates change; the right lane updates an account card and sequence counter.
modules.interaction: Complete the same fictional transfer once in each lane without double-spending or reusing a sequence number.

checkQuestion.prompt: Which model consumes a referenced output only once?

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: a UTXO becomes spent when a valid transaction consumes it.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Watch the discrete output disappear and new outputs take its place.
modules.recap: UTXO replaces outputs; accounts mutate ordered state.
modules.simplificationNotes: The transfer uses fictional units and omits scripts, byte formats, and protocol fees.

modules.title: Proof of Work and Proof of Stake
modules.learnerGoal: Distinguish computational-work fork choice from stake-based validator selection and voting.
modules.verifiedConcept: Proof of Work and Proof of Stake are Sybil-resistance and consensus families with different resources, actors, and failure assumptions.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: PoW compares accumulated computational work; PoS protocols select and hold staked validators accountable under specific voting rules.
modules.demonstration: A hash-target lane and an attestation-threshold lane progress with visibly different states.
modules.interaction: Place work proofs and validator attestations into their correct lanes, then reject a mixed mechanism card.

checkQuestion.prompt: Is Proof of Stake one identical algorithm on every network?
answers: No, it is a family of protocol designs

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: concrete PoS protocols define different selection, voting, and finality rules.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Treat PoS as a family, then inspect a named protocol example.
modules.recap: PoW and PoS secure agreement through different protocol-defined resources and rules.
modules.simplificationNotes: No energy, reward, profitability, or current validator parameter is simulated.

modules.title: Smart contracts and gas
modules.learnerGoal: Explain deterministic contract execution, resource metering, and transaction reversion.
modules.verifiedConcept: Ethereum contract calls execute deterministic code under gas metering and may commit state or revert.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: A contract is a rule machine, and gas bounds how much computation and state work its transaction may request.
modules.demonstration: A call crosses three operation tolls; one branch completes and another runs out of abstract gas then rolls back.
modules.interaction: Choose a route that satisfies the contract condition within eight fictional gas cells.

checkQuestion.prompt: Does a reverted execution mean no computational resources were used?
answers: No, execution can consume gas before reverting

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: rollback of state does not imply free computation.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Watch the resource meter before the rollback wave restores state.
modules.recap: Gas meters execution; valid state changes commit, while failed calls can revert.
modules.simplificationNotes: Gas cells are abstract and never represent a live price, token amount, or fee quote.

modules.title: Rollups, privacy, and interoperability
modules.learnerGoal: Separate scaling proofs, privacy proofs, data availability, and cross-chain message verification.
modules.verifiedConcept: Optimistic rollups use fault challenges, ZK rollups use validity proofs, and cross-chain messages require their own source-authentication model.
modules.oneSentenceExplanation: A proof can validate a batched state transition without automatically hiding data or authenticating a different chain's message.
modules.demonstration: Three gates accept different evidence: a fault trace, a validity proof, and an IBC-style packet proof with sequence.
modules.interaction: Route each evidence card to its correct gate and reject the claim that every ZK rollup is private.

checkQuestion.prompt: Does 'ZK rollup' automatically mean all transaction data is private?
answers: No, validity and privacy are separate design properties

modules.correctFeedback: Correct: zero-knowledge technology can serve validity without making the whole system private.
modules.incorrectFeedback: Separate what the proof establishes from what data the protocol publishes.
modules.recap: Scaling, privacy, and interoperability use different proofs and security assumptions.
modules.simplificationNotes: The three gates compare concepts and do not model a universal rollup, privacy protocol, or bridge.
